metadata.sources: PCA labor surveys 2025-2026, Sherwin-Williams trade pricing, Benjamin Moore trade pricing, PPG/Behr/Valspar contractor pricing, RSMeans residential 2026, Painting Contractor Magazine cost surveys

paintTiers.label: Builder / contractor grade
paintTiers.notes: Acceptable for low-traffic interior. Limited washability. Used for flips and rentals.

paintTiers.label: Mid-range
paintTiers.notes: 100% acrylic, washable, good hide. Standard for most residential repaints.

paintTiers.label: Premium
paintTiers.notes: Ceramic-microsphere or hybrid resins. Excellent washability and stain resistance. Lifetime warranty common.

paintTiers.label: Ultra-premium / specialty
paintTiers.notes: Best hide (often 1-coat coverage), best washability, longest fade resistance. Required for deep saturated colors and luxury finishes.

paintTiers.label: Exterior premium (UV / weather)
paintTiers.notes: Designed for full sun and freeze-thaw. Lifetime warranty common.

paintTiers.label: Exterior elastomeric (stucco)
paintTiers.notes: Bridges hairline cracks in stucco. Long life on Mediterranean/Southwest homes.

laborRates.notes: Labor rates assume full prep (light scrape, spot prime, caulk, mask). Heavy prep (full strip, lead abatement, repair) is billed separately. Spray-and-back-roll on exterior is 2-3x faster than brush-and-roll but adds masking time.

interior_single_room.label: Interior single room (12x12 walls only, mid-tier paint)
interior_single_room.scope: Walls 2 coats, light prep, 1 accent wall optional. Trim and ceiling not included.

interior_whole_house.label: Interior whole house (1,500-2,500 sqft, walls + trim)
interior_whole_house.scope: All living rooms, bedrooms, halls. 2 coats. Standard prep.

exterior_body_only.label: Exterior body only (1,500-2,500 sqft wall area)
exterior_body_only.scope: Body only. Trim, doors, shutters separate.

exterior_full.label: Exterior whole house (body + trim + doors + shutters)
exterior_full.notes: Spray-and-back-roll on body, brush trim. Light scrape and prime over peeling areas.

exterior_full_heavy_prep.label: Exterior whole house with heavy prep (peeling, repairs, lead abatement)
exterior_full_heavy_prep.notes: Pre-1978 homes need RRP-certified contractor. Full strip on areas with widespread peeling can double the labor.

cabinet_repaint.label: Cabinet repaint (15-25 doors)
cabinet_repaint.scope: Sand, prime, 2 coats. Brush-and-roll or sprayed in shop. Spraying gives factory finish.

permitCosts.notes: Painting itself rarely requires a permit. Pre-1978 lead RRP work requires a certified firm and lead-safe work practices but not a building permit in most jurisdictions.

stateLicensing.notes: Painting contractors are licensed in some states under the general contractor license; in others under a specialty home improvement license; in some, no license is required.
stateLicensing.epaRRPRequired: ALL states for pre-1978 paint disturbance

codeReferences.leadRRP: EPA RRP rule (40 CFR 745) — required for paid renovations on pre-1978 homes that disturb more than 6 sqft interior or 20 sqft exterior. Fines up to $37,500/day per violation.
codeReferences.vocLimits: EPA AIM rule and CARB SCM in CA limit VOC content. Most consumer paints are now compliant.
codeReferences.encapsulation: Pre-1978 homes can use lead-encapsulant paints (Fiberlock LBC) where abatement is impractical.

redFlagPatterns.explanation: Pre-1978 home or peeling paint mentioned with no EPA RRP certification. Federal law requires RRP-certified contractors for paint-disturbing work on pre-1978 homes. Fines start at $37,500/day per violation. Verify the firm at epa.gov/lead.

Paint line item with no brand/line specified. Same square footage costs $400-$1,200/room depending on whether the painter uses ProMar 200 ($28/gal) or Emerald ($90/gal). Always specify brand AND product line.

Quote specifies a single coat. Acceptable only with ultra-premium 1-coat-coverage products (Emerald, Aura) and only over a similar existing color. For color changes, dark-to-light, or any production work, 2 coats is the standard.

No mention of prep. Painting is 70% prep, 30% paint. Quotes that don't describe prep are either lowballing (the prep happens later as a 'change order') or planning to skip it (paint will fail in 1-2 years).
Exterior paint job with no power wash mentioned. Mandatory prep step. Paint over dirt, mildew, or chalk fails within a year. Skipping it is grounds to walk away.

Exterior spray only with no back-rolling mentioned. Spray-only on exterior surfaces deposits paint on top rather than working it into the surface; lifespan is 50-70% shorter. Reputable exterior crews always back-roll behind the sprayer.
Labor rate below $25/hr is well below 2026 prevailing wage for licensed painters in nearly every market. Suggests undocumented labor, no insurance, or no taxes paid.
No workmanship warranty stated. Reputable painters offer 2-7 year workmanship warranties depending on tier. No warranty signals no confidence in the work.

No mention of liability insurance or workers' comp. Painters fall off ladders. Without workers' comp the homeowner can be liable for injuries on their property. Always require a Certificate of Insurance naming you as additional insured.
Deposits above 25% on residential painting are high risk. Reputable painters take 10-25% deposit and stage payments by completed area or phase.
Workmanship warranty shorter than 1 year. Industry minimum at the legitimate end is 1 year, with 2-7 years standard at the mid-tier.

Exterior paint scheduled in cold months. Most consumer paints fail to cure below 50F (some 'cold weather' formulations work to 35F). Avoid scheduling exteriors November-March in northern climates unless using a verified cold-weather product.
Elastomeric coating quoted on a non-stucco surface. Elastomeric is designed for masonry and traps moisture in wood, leading to rot. Wrong product for the substrate.
No color matching or consultation noted. Reputable painters provide sample boards or in-place color drawdowns before committing to gallons.

scopeCheckItems.label: Paint brand AND product line specified
EPA RRP certified for pre-1978 homes

iraCredit.notes: Painting does not qualify for federal energy credits.

questionsToAskContractor: What brand and product line of paint will you use, and how many coats?
What does prep include — sanding, caulking, patching, priming?
Is the home pre-1978? If so, are you EPA RRP certified?
For exterior, do you power wash first and back-roll behind the sprayer?
Can you provide a Certificate of Insurance naming me as additional insured?
What is the deposit and payment schedule?
Will you provide a sample board or test patch before final color commitment?
What is your plan for adjacent floors, furniture, and landscaping protection?
How many painters will be on site and how many days?
